summary.brief: Patient developed bowel perforation after XLIF spinal surgery. Post-operative signs of perforation (pain, fever) were dismissed as normal surgical responses, delaying diagnosis by 3 days.
summary.clinical_synopsis: Female patient underwent extreme lateral intrabody fusion (XLIF) procedure for back pain. On post-op day 1, she developed pain and fever which were dismissed as normal surgical responses. A second planned surgery was performed on post-op day 2. On post-op day 3, she developed incision site redness with elevated temperature and blood pressure, prompting CT scan that revealed free air and material outside the colon. Emergency surgery found sigmoid colon perforation with stool leakage. Patient required four additional surgeries and 30-day hospitalization, resulting in permanent medical conditions.
summary.legal_synopsis: Case reversed and remanded by Michigan Supreme Court on procedural grounds (expert witness admissibility). The underlying malpractice allegations concern failure to recognize post-operative signs of bowel perforation and dismissing warning symptoms as normal surgical responses.

evidence_index.text: Dr. Fahim and Dr. D'Andrea performed an extreme lateral intrabody fusion (XLIF) procedure on December 7, 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff experienced pain and fever the next day. Dr. Fahim believed those complications were normal surgical responses and did not have concerns.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He performed the second spinal surgery on December 9, 2015, thus concluding the XLIF operation as initially planned.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On December 10, 2015, plaintiff experienced redness at the site of the incision that was made during the first procedure. Plaintiff's body temperature and blood pressure both elevated to the point that she was taken to the hospital's intensive care unit for a CT scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The CT scan revealed that there was 'free air and free material outside the colon.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During this procedure, Dr. Anthony Iacco observed that there was a hole in plaintiff's sigmoid colon that was leaking stool.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff had four surgeries over the next six days to correct the perforation. She was discharged from the hospital on January 6, 2016—30 days after her first procedure. She complains of continuing medical ramifications that have now become permanent.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Koebbe averred that the appropriate standard of care includes: to diagnose and surgically repair all injuries to the patient's colon prior to closing the surgical site.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert (Dr. Koebbe)

evidence_index.text: Perforations to the colon that occur during lumbar spine surgery are extremely rare. It happens less than one percent of the time.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert (Dr. Koebbe)

evidence_index.text: A bowel injury caused during the type of procedure performed upon Ms. Danhoff is not an acceptable known complication of this procedure but rather is so rare as to only occur as a result of surgical error.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert (Dr. Koebbe)

evidence_index.text: Reversed and remanded. The trial court abused its discretion by inadequately assessing the reliability of a standard-of-care expert witness.
evidence_index.speaker: Michigan Supreme Court

simulation.testable_reason: Clear post-operative monitoring failure with dismissal of warning signs

initial_state.chief_complaint: Pain and fever one day after spinal surgery
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient underwent XLIF (extreme lateral intrabody fusion) spinal surgery yesterday for back pain from a work injury. The surgery was performed on the L3-L4 disc space. Today she is experiencing pain and fever.
past_medical_history: Work-related back injury requiring surgical intervention
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Post-operative pain and fever after XLIF procedure
initial_state.known_constraints: Patient is 1 day post-op from lateral approach spinal surgery near retroperitoneal space

requestables.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
requestables.description: CT scan of abdomen and pelvis with contrast to evaluate for intra-abdominal complications

reveal.result_summary: Free air and free material outside the colon
reveal.detailed_findings: Pneumoperitoneum with extraluminal material adjacent to sigmoid colon, concerning for bowel perforation
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates bowel perforation requiring emergency surgical intervention
reveal.decision_impact: Would have prompted immediate surgical exploration and repair
reveal.if_normal_would_mean: No evidence of bowel injury, pain/fever likely from other cause

requestables.name: CBC with differential
requestables.description: Complete blood count to assess for infection/inflammation

reveal.result_summary: Likely elevated WBC with left shift
Potentially elevated in setting of bowel ischemia/perforation
reveal.clinical_significance: Elevated lactate in post-op patient with fever raises concern for serious complication

key_events.event: XLIF procedure performed by Dr. Fahim and Dr. D'Andrea

timeline_phases.name: Post-operative Day 1 - CRITICAL DECISION POINT
timeline_phases.description: Patient develops pain and fever - dismissed as normal

key_events.event: Dr. Fahim dismisses symptoms as normal surgical responses

timeline_phases.name: Post-operative Day 2
timeline_phases.description: Second planned surgery performed despite symptoms

key_events.event: Second spinal surgery performed as planned

timeline_phases.name: Post-operative Day 3 - Deterioration
timeline_phases.description: Patient deteriorates with clear signs of complication

key_events.event: Incision site redness, elevated temperature and blood pressure
Transfer to ICU, CT scan performed
CT reveals free air and material outside colon

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Surgical Intervention
timeline_phases.description: Discovery and repair of bowel perforation

key_events.event: Sigmoid colon perforation with stool leakage discovered
Four additional surgeries to correct perforation

decision_points.clinical_context: Post-operative day 1 after XLIF spinal surgery. Patient has developed pain and fever. XLIF is performed through a lateral retroperitoneal approach, in proximity to the sigmoid colon.
decision_points.prompt: A patient presents with pain and fever on post-operative day 1 after XLIF spinal surgery. How should you evaluate and manage these symptoms?

options.description: Order CT abdomen/pelvis to rule out intra-abdominal injury given proximity of surgical approach to bowel
options.clinical_reasoning: XLIF approaches the spine through the retroperitoneal space near the sigmoid colon. Fever and pain post-operatively should raise concern for bowel injury, which requires prompt imaging

options.description: Dismiss as normal post-operative inflammatory response and proceed with planned second surgery
options.clinical_reasoning: Failed to consider serious complication despite proximity of surgical approach to bowel structures

actual_action_defendant.description: Dismissed pain and fever as normal surgical responses and proceeded with second planned surgery
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Dr. Fahim believed those complications were normal surgical responses and did not have concerns

expected_action_court.description: Should have investigated post-operative fever and pain with imaging to rule out bowel injury, given the surgical approach passes near the sigmoid colon
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony: Standard of care requires diagnosing and repairing injuries to bowel. Post-op fever and pain after retroperitoneal surgery warrants evaluation for bowel injury.

criteria: Recognizes need for imaging workup (CT) given symptoms
Considers bowel injury in differential for post-XLIF fever/pain
Does not dismiss symptoms as routine without investigation

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Dismissed post-operative fever and pain as normal without considering that XLIF surgery passes near the sigmoid colon, creating risk of bowel injury. Failed to order imaging that would have revealed perforation 2 days earlier.
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Post-operative fever and pain after retroperitoneal spinal surgery should prompt evaluation for intra-abdominal complications including bowel injury. CT imaging should have been ordered on post-op day 1.
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Expert testimony established that bowel perforation during XLIF is extremely rare (<1%) and when it occurs, represents surgical error. Standard of care requires prompt diagnosis.

decision_points.reasoning_error_explanation: Attributed symptoms to benign cause (normal post-op response) without adequately considering more serious alternatives
decision_points.what_triggered_error: Anchoring on expectation that symptoms were normal post-operative course

patient_outcome.description: Sigmoid colon perforation discovered 3 days post-op. Required four additional surgeries, ostomy, 30-day hospitalization, and developed permanent medical conditions.
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Permanent medical conditions from delayed treatment of bowel perforation

legal_outcome.damages_awarded: Not determined - case remanded for retrial
key_findings: Michigan Supreme Court reversed lower court's exclusion of plaintiff's expert witness
Case remanded for retrial on malpractice claims

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to investigate post-operative fever and pain with appropriate imaging, resulting in 2-day delay in diagnosis of bowel perforation
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier CT imaging would have revealed perforation sooner, potentially reducing number of required surgeries and severity of permanent disability

factual_timeline.event: First XLIF surgery performed on L3-L4
Patient develops pain and fever - dismissed as normal post-op response
Patient deteriorates - redness at incision, elevated temp/BP, transferred to ICU
CT scan reveals free air and material outside colon
Emergency surgery reveals sigmoid colon perforation with stool leakage
Four additional surgeries to repair perforation and complications

tests_performed.result: Free air and free material outside the colon

complications.when_occurred: During or immediately after December 7, 2015 surgery

allegations.allegation: Failure to diagnose bowel perforation in timely manner
Dismissing post-operative warning signs as normal

court_findings.finding: Trial court improperly excluded plaintiff's expert witness
court_findings.legal_significance: Case reversed and remanded for retrial

malpractice_analysis.key_teaching_point: Post-operative fever and pain after retroperitoneal spinal surgery should prompt investigation for bowel injury. XLIF approaches pass near the sigmoid colon, and bowel perforation, while rare (<1%), requires prompt diagnosis to minimize harm.

missing_elements: Patient age not specified in judgment
Specific vital sign values not documented
warnings: Case was reversed and remanded - no final liability determination

extraction_metadata.revision_notes: Manually revised to reframe as post-operative monitoring scenario. Original extraction incorrectly focused on intraoperative technique. Key teaching point is recognition of post-op warning signs of bowel perforation after retroperitoneal surgery.
